IMPACT.justification: This paper addresses an important evaluation gap in GEC by proposing a dataset-independent, rule-based method to automatically label edits with one of 55 error types, enabling detailed per-error-type evaluation. The tool is validated (human experts judged predicted categories as "Good" or "Acceptable" for ≥95% of cases) and demonstrated on CoNLL-2014 system outputs to reveal complementary system strengths, suggesting it will change comparative evaluation practices. Code availability and the method’s reliance on standard linguistic preprocessing make it likely to be adopted and built upon, though the small-scale manual validation and preprocessing requirements somewhat limit immediate universality.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper presents a notable, dataset-independent rule-based method for automatically annotating GEC edits with a fine-grained (55-type) taxonomy, which is a clear methodological contribution beyond prior M2/MaxEnt approaches. Its two-step linguistically-enhanced alignment plus deterministic classifier is a non-trivial technical alternative to existing classifiers and enables per-error-type evaluation at scale. The authors also differentiate their work from prior methods and validate category quality with expert judgments, so the contribution would inform experts though it is not a radical theoretical breakthrough.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claims are stated and supported by experiments and a small human evaluation (200 edits rated by 5 researchers) and by application to CoNLL-2014 outputs with comparisons to M2 Scorer and prior MaxEnt classifiers, showing meaningful per-error-type analyses. No mathematical derivations were required and none are missing. However, the manual evaluation is small-scale and hyperparameters are not reported, which weakens claims about general reliability and reproducibility. Overall the methodology and evidence are sound but with minor shortcomings that do not invalidate the main conclusions.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well-organized and describes a clear two-step method (linguistically-enhanced alignment + deterministic rule-based classifier) with its main contributions stated early. Notation and methodological description are consistent (55 error types, use of POS/lemmatization/dependency parses) and writing quality is reported as good, with relevant metrics and datasets presented. Minor presentation gaps remain—manual evaluation is small-scale (200 edits, 5 researchers) and hyperparameters are not reported—which slightly limits completeness/replicability but do not substantially harm clarity.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic—automatic annotation and per-error-type evaluation for Grammatical Error Correction—is squarely within the scope and interest of a top NLP/ML venue. The paper targets the right audience and uses appropriate problem formulation: a dataset-independent, rule-based classifier built on automatic linguistic preprocessing (spaCy, lemmatization, stemming, dependency parses) to label 55 error types. Methods and evaluation follow community expectations by comparing to established baselines (M2 scorer, prior MaxEnt classifiers), reporting standard metrics (F0.5, F-score, detection, κfree) and including human expert judgments, while noted limitations (dependency on preprocessing and a small manual evaluation of 200 edits) do not undermine its relevance or fit.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper provides solid experimental support: evaluations on CoNLL-2014 and FCE-test, comparisons to M2 and MaxEnt baselines, and application to CoNLL-2014 system outputs to demonstrate per-error-type behavior. However, ablation or sensitivity analyses of the rule-based classifier and the alignment components are not reported, and hyperparameter details are missing, limiting analysis of robustness. Related work coverage is described as adequate and the authors explicitly note limitations (required automatic preprocessing; small-scale manual evaluation of 200 edits), and code availability aids reproducibility despite some missing detail.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against appropriate baselines (M2 Scorer and prior Maximum Entropy classifiers from Swanson & Yamangil 2012 and Xue & Hwa 2014) on standard GEC datasets (CoNLL-2014, FCE-test) using common metrics (F0.5, F-score, detection), and related work coverage is described as adequate. However, the summary does not state whether all experimental conditions (preprocessing, hyperparameters) were exactly matched to ensure fully fair comparisons, nor does it explicitly report acknowledging cases where prior methods perform comparably or better. Overall, the comparisons are good and relevant but omit some fairness/acknowledgement details.

main_claims: Proposes a dataset-independent, rule-based method to automatically annotate grammatical error corrections with error types, enabling detailed per-error-type evaluation of system output.

Uses a two-step approach combining a linguistically-enhanced alignment to extract edits and a deterministic rule-based classifier (based on automatic POS, lemmatization, stemming, dependency parses and other automatic mark-up) to assign one of 55 error types.
Reports that human expert ratings judged the predicted error categories as "Good" or "Acceptable" in at least 95% of cases (small-scale evaluation of 200 edits by 5 researchers).

Applies the tool to CoNLL-2014 shared task system outputs to produce detailed analyses showing different systems have complementary strengths across error types (e.g., CAMB strong overall; CUUI strong on certain replacement determiner errors).
_summary.methodology: Extract edits between original and corrected sentences using a linguistically-enhanced alignment, then classify each edit with a deterministic, rule-based classifier that uses automatic POS tags, lemmata, stems, dependency parses and other automatic mark-up (spaCy, Lancaster Stemmer, Hunspell).
baselines: Maximum Entropy classifiers (Swanson and Yamangil 2012; Xue and Hwa 2014)
limitations: Requires automatic linguistic preprocessing for each token (POS tagging, lemmatization, stemming, dependency parsing).
Manual evaluation was small-scale (5 researchers rating 200 randomly chosen edits).
